mnemonics: More Pigeons Than Holes: If you have more pigeons (items) than pigeonholes (containers), at least one hole must contain more than one pigeon.
keyPoints: The principle states that if n items are put into m containers, with n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item.
It is a powerful tool for proving the existence of a property without necessarily constructing it.
It is often applied in combinatorial mathematics, number theory, and computer science to prove that a certain configuration is inevitable.
commonMistakes: Assuming it provides a specific count of items in a container; it only proves that at least one container has more than one.
Misapplying the principle by not ensuring that n > m, which is the fundamental requirement for it to hold.
